text: The video displays images of a herniation in the right inguinal canal, with visible involvement of the bladder.

The surgeon begins the robotic inguinal hernia repair, opting to dissect slightly above the previous mesh to avoid heavy scarring. The approach is lateral, carefully avoiding the cord structures and epigastric vessels. The mesh is visibly adhered to the peritoneum and surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon identifies the reherniation in the inferior and medial positions of the mesh, with the bladder involved. Using a cystoscope, the urologist assists in reducing the bladder from the hernia sac with significant traction and counter traction. The robotic camera and arms facilitate the tedious process, eventually allowing the complete reduction of the bladder, verified by cystoscopy. The bladder is transected from the hernia sac, and ICG is used to identify the epigastric vessels.

The surgeon demonstrates the anatomical defect and direct space on the patient's right side, preparing to place a 3D Max right-sided inguinal mesh. The mesh is affixed in two or three spots using absorbable Vial sutures, secured at the pubic tubercle and anteriorly on either side of the epigastric vessels. The peritoneum is closed with a 3-0 V-lock suture, completely covering the mesh to prevent migration and provide separation from the intestines. Gas is suctioned out of the peritoneal cavity.
And the patient did very well, was discharged home postoperative day zero with a Foley catheter to remain in place for ten days.
